meta.description: ALL enriched hunt cards — compressed detection rules with verification and triage context for direct bug validation
meta.usage: Load this single file for all vulnerability detection cards. For each card: grep target code for card.grep. On hit: execute card.check steps directly against grep hit locations, use validWhen/invalidWhen/impact for rapid triage, and only read full DB entry (card.ref + card.lines) for confirmed true/likely positives.

cards.title: ERC-7579 Module System — Registry Bypass, moduleType Confusion, Hook PostCheck Skip, Fallback Flaws
cards.detect: ERC-7579 module management has multiple independent failure modes:
check: VERIFY: ERC-7579 module management has multiple independent failure modes
PROVE: *Scenario A — Registry bypass via compromised module [MOD-1]; Attacker submits a malicious module that passes ERC-7484 attestation checks at install time.
ANTIPATTERN: Fallback handler can be called directly by anyone via the fallback function
cards.antipattern: Fallback handler can be called directly by anyone via the fallback function
cards.validWhen: ERC-7579 module management has multiple independent failure modes
preconditions: Attacker submits a malicious module that passes ERC-7484 attestation checks at install time.
Account owner signs an enable-mode approval for module M as MODULE_TYPE_VALIDATOR.
Attacker intercepts and re-submits the same enableModeSignature but with moduleType = MODULE_TYPE_EXECUTOR.
Attacker or buggy module reverts at onUninstall(address, bytes).
Account owner cannot remove the module through the normal uninstall path.
cards.proofShape: *Scenario A — Registry bypass via compromised module [MOD-1] -> Attacker submits a malicious module that passes ERC-7484 attestation checks at install time.

cards.title: AA Paymaster Gas Accounting — Prefund Errors, Duplicate Snapshots, Stake Bypass, and Fee Escape
cards.detect: Gas accounting in ERC-4337 is a multi-phase process (validation → execution → postOp) with several hand-off points...
check: VERIFY: Gas accounting in ERC-4337 is a multi-phase process (validation → execution → postOp) with several hand-off points where gas values can be measured...
PROVE: *Scenario A — Inverted prefund multiplier [GAS-1] (OpenZeppelin EIP-4337 audit, HIGH); An op WITHOUT a paymaster submits verificationGasLimit = G.
ANTIPATTERN: preGas reused from before-validation for before-postOp measurement
cards.antipattern: preGas reused from before-validation for before-postOp measurement
cards.validWhen: Gas accounting in ERC-4337 is a multi-phase process (validation → execution → postOp) with several hand-off points where gas values can be measured incorrectly
preconditions: Non-paymaster ops receive no reserve multiplier → under-funded → EntryPoint reverts or attacker pays less gas than expected.
Attacker deploys contract where paymaster == smartAccount.
deposit(paymaster, amount) internally calls paymaster.addStake() using locked stake balance.
Attacker crafts a userOp with a manipulated paymasterAndData field (e.g., inflated token refund amount).
cards.proofShape: *Scenario A — Inverted prefund multiplier [GAS-1] (OpenZeppelin EIP-4337 audit, HIGH) -> An op WITHOUT a paymaster submits verificationGasLimit = G.

cards.title: Session Key Abuse — Spend Limit Bypass, Cross-Wallet Consumption, Permission Overwrite, PermissionId Frontrun
cards.detect: Session key security depends on correct enforcement of four invariants:
check: VERIFY: Session key security depends on correct enforcement of four invariants
PROVE: *Scenario A — Session key drains entire wallet [SK-1] (Shieldify, CRITICAL); Alice's smart wallet has a CredibleAccountModule with a session key SK authorized for...
ANTIPATTERN: permissionId decoded from userOp.signature which is NOT signed by EntryPoint
cards.antipattern: permissionId decoded from userOp.signature which is NOT signed by EntryPoint
cards.validWhen: Session key security depends on correct enforcement of four invariants
preconditions: SK owner calls ERC20.approve(attacker, type(uint256).max) on every token in the wallet.
Attacker calls ERC20.transferFrom(aliceWallet, attacker, balance) to drain all tokens.
SK_A owner signs a userOp but submits it claiming to be SK_B (or vice-versa).
Bundler/attacker sees the pending transaction and replaces permissionId in userOp.signature with their own permissionId = Y...
Alice's policies get installed on permissionId = Y instead of X — attacker gains Alice's policy permissions.
cards.proofShape: *Scenario A — Session key drains entire wallet [SK-1] (Shieldify, CRITICAL) -> Alice's smart wallet has a CredibleAccountModule with a session key SK authorized for specific locked tokens.

cards.title: Vulnerability Title
cards.detect: This vulnerability exists because **swap operations set amountOutMinimum to 0 or use ineffective slippage...
check: VERIFY: Signal 1: Transaction can be frontrun by MEV bots observing the mempool; Signal 2: No commit-reveal or private mempool protection for sensitive...
PROVE: *Scenario 1: Classic Sandwich Attack (Most Common - 43/43 reports); Alice submits a swap transaction with inadequate slippage protection (amountOutMinimum: 0)
FALSIFY: Not this bug when: Transaction uses private mempool (Flashbots) or commit-reveal scheme; Safe if: Slippage protection with reasonable bounds is enforced
IMPACT: Fund Extraction: MEV bots extract value from every unprotected swap (15/43 reports)
All swap operations have non-zero amountOutMinimum or equivalent
All deadline parameters use user-specified values, not block.timestamp
cards.antipattern: deadline set to max uint256 effectively disables protection
cards.securePattern: Use TWAP oracle for slippage bounds (not spot price)
cards.validWhen: Signal 1: Transaction can be frontrun by MEV bots observing the mempool; Signal 2: No commit-reveal or private mempool protection for sensitive operations
cards.invalidWhen: Not this bug when: Transaction uses private mempool (Flashbots) or commit-reveal scheme; Safe if: Slippage protection with reasonable bounds is enforced
cards.impact: Fund Extraction: MEV bots extract value from every unprotected swap (15/43 reports)
preconditions: attacker control of: specific conditions per pattern
Attacker detects incoming swap transaction
Attacker adds concentrated liquidity at the target tick, capturing ~99% of active liquidity
Attacker receives majority of penalty fee, withdraws liquidity for profit
cards.proofShape: *Scenario 1: Classic Sandwich Attack (Most Common - 43/43 reports) -> Alice submits a swap transaction with inadequate slippage protection (amountOutMinimum: 0) -> MEV bot detects pending transaction in mempool
falsePositiveSignals: Not this bug when: Transaction uses private mempool (Flashbots) or commit-reveal scheme
Safe if: Slippage protection with reasonable bounds is enforced
Requires attacker control of: specific conditions per pattern

cards.title: 2. Slippage Protection Vulnerabilities
cards.detect: Functions that swap tokens or add/remove liquidity don't validate that the received amount meets user expectations.
check: VERIFY: Functions that swap tokens or add/remove liquidity don't validate that the received amount meets user expectations.
PROVE: User submits swap: 100 ETH → USDC, expects ~$200,000; MEV bot sees pending transaction
FALSIFY: Compare against oracle price
IMPACT: Users receive significantly less tokens than expected
cards.antipattern: Slippage parameter ignored in execution
cards.validWhen: Functions that swap tokens or add/remove liquidity don't validate that the received amount meets user expectations.
cards.impact: Users receive significantly less tokens than expected
cards.proofShape: User submits swap: 100 ETH → USDC, expects ~$200,000 -> MEV bot sees pending transaction -> Bot front-runs: buys USDC, pushing price up

cards.title: 26. Incorrect Liquidity Ownership Assumptions
cards.detect:
Protocol calculates:

```

lpToRemove = (tokenToRemove * totalLpSupply) / totalReserve

```



But if protocol only owns a fraction of the pool, `lpToRemove` exceeds protocol's LP balance.
check: VERIFY: lpToRemove = (tokenToRemove * totalLpSupply) / totalReserve
PROVE: Uses total pool reserves, not owned portion
FALSIFY: Base calculations on protocol's actual LP holdings
cards.antipattern: Uses total pool reserves, not owned portion
cards.securePattern: Base calculations on protocol's actual LP holdings
cards.validWhen: lpToRemove = (tokenToRemove * totalLpSupply) / totalReserve
cards.invalidWhen: Base calculations on protocol's actual LP holdings
cards.proofShape: Uses total pool reserves, not owned portion

cards.title: Vulnerability Title
cards.detect: UUPS proxy vulnerabilities arise from several fundamental issues:
check: VERIFY: Signal 1: Storage slot collision between proxy and implementation contracts; Signal 2: Upgrade changes storage layout order, corrupting existing state
PROVE: *Scenario 1: Uninitialized Implementation Takeover (MEDIUM-HIGH); Protocol deploys UUPS proxy with OZ 4.3.1 but forgets to initialize implementation contract
FALSIFY: Not this bug when: Storage gaps used in all upgradeable base contracts; Safe if: Upgrade tested with storage layout comparison tooling
IMPACT: Implementation Takeover: Attacker can initialize uninitialized implementation contracts (OZ 4.1.0-4.3.1)
Verify OpenZeppelin version >= 4.3.2 or implementation has _disableInitializers()
Check deployment scripts initialize implementation contracts
cards.antipattern: Implementation contract not initialized after deployment
cards.validWhen: Signal 1: Storage slot collision between proxy and implementation contracts; Signal 2: Upgrade changes storage layout order, corrupting existing state
cards.invalidWhen: Not this bug when: Storage gaps used in all upgradeable base contracts; Safe if: Upgrade tested with storage layout comparison tooling
cards.impact: Implementation Takeover: Attacker can initialize uninitialized implementation contracts (OZ 4.1.0-4.3.1)
preconditions: attacker control of: specific conditions per pattern
Attacker calls initialize() on implementation contract directly (not through proxy)
Attacker becomes owner of implementation contract
Attacker calls upgradeTo() or upgradeToAndCall() with malicious implementation
Attacker can selfdestruct implementation, bricking all proxies pointing to it
cards.proofShape: *Scenario 1: Uninitialized Implementation Takeover (MEDIUM-HIGH) -> Protocol deploys UUPS proxy with OZ 4.3.1 but forgets to initialize implementation contract -> Attacker calls initialize() on implementation contract...
falsePositiveSignals: Not this bug when: Storage gaps used in all upgradeable base contracts
Safe if: Upgrade tested with storage layout comparison tooling
Requires attacker control of: specific conditions per pattern

cards.title: Vulnerability Title
cards.detect: Diamond proxy vulnerabilities arise from several fundamental issues:
check: VERIFY: Signal 1: Storage slot collision between proxy and implementation contracts; Signal 2: Upgrade changes storage layout order, corrupting existing state
PROVE: *Scenario 1: Storage Collision Leading to System Failure (HIGH); Protocol upgrades Diamond with new struct that changes size from 7 to 2 slots
FALSIFY: Not this bug when: Storage gaps used in all upgradeable base contracts; Safe if: Upgrade tested with storage layout comparison tooling
IMPACT: Storage Corruption: All state variables after modified struct read incorrect data (3/13 reports)
Verify storage layout preserved when upgrading structs (use Slither `--print variable-order`)
Confirm all facets use EIP-7201 namespaced storage or gap patterns
cards.antipattern: Changing struct size shifts all subsequent storage
cards.securePattern: Mappings allow safe extension of inner structures
cards.validWhen: Signal 1: Storage slot collision between proxy and implementation contracts; Signal 2: Upgrade changes storage layout order, corrupting existing state
cards.invalidWhen: Not this bug when: Storage gaps used in all upgradeable base contracts; Safe if: Upgrade tested with storage layout comparison tooling
cards.impact: Storage Corruption: All state variables after modified struct read incorrect data (3/13 reports)
preconditions: attacker control of: specific conditions per pattern
In callback, attacker calls advancedFarm{value: 1 wei} with empty data
Execution falls through to ETH refund; entire Diamond balance sent to attacker
cards.proofShape: *Scenario 1: Storage Collision Leading to System Failure (HIGH) -> Protocol upgrades Diamond with new struct that changes size from 7 to 2 slots -> All subsequent variables in AppStorage shift their storage positions
falsePositiveSignals: Not this bug when: Storage gaps used in all upgradeable base contracts
Safe if: Upgrade tested with storage layout comparison tooling
Requires attacker control of: specific conditions per pattern

cards.title: Pattern 2: Incorrect Randomness Computation Allows Proof Forgery

check: VERIFY: In interactive proof systems converted to non-interactive via Fiat-Shamir, the "random" challenge must be derived from all previous transcript values.
PROVE: Prover generates multiple commitments and includes them in the transcript; The Fiat-Shamir challenge is computed as H(C1, C2, ...) but due to a bug, some Ci are excluded
FALSIFY: Include ALL public commitments in the Fiat-Shamir transcript
ANTIPATTERN: Incorrect randomness — missing some commitments in transcript

cards.invalidWhen: Include ALL public commitments in the Fiat-Shamir transcript
preconditions: Attacker produces a valid-looking proof for an incorrect statement

ANTIPATTERN: Dutch auction price depends on block.timestamp

cards.securePattern: Check sequencer is up (and has been up for grace period) before auctions
cards.validWhen: Dutch auction price depends on block.

cards.proofShape: Protocol runs Dutch auctions (price decreases linearly over time) or time-limited options -> L2 sequencer goes down during an active auction/option period -> When sequencer comes back online, auction start price was...
